title: 第 46 節
en: PHILOSOPHY AND RELIGION. 325 before he had returned to the Devas, he saw this difficulty. In truth he thus does not know himself (his self) that he is I, nor does he know anything that exists. He is gone to utter annihilation. I see no good in this.' i .
' Taking fuel in his hand he went again as a pupil to Pra^apati. Pra^apati said to him : " Maghavat, as you went away satisfied in your heart, for what purpose did you come back 1 "
1 He said : " Sir, in that way he does not know himself (his self) that he is I, nor does he know anything that exists. He is gone to utter annihila tion. I see no good in this."
I shall explain him (the true Self) further to you, and nothing more than this1. Live here other five years."

' He lived there other five years. This made in all one hundred and one years, and therefore it is said that Indra Maghavat lived one hundred and one years as a pupil with Pra^apati. Praf/apati said to him :

' " Maghavat, this body is mortal and always held by death. It is the abode of that Self which is immortal and without body 2. When in the body (by thinking this body is I and I am this body) the Self is held by pleasure and pain. So long as he is in 1 /Sankara explains this as meaning, the real Self, not anything different from the Self.
2 According to some, the body is the result of the Self, the elements of the body, light, water, and earth springing from the Self, and the Self afterwards entering them.
the body, he cannot get free from pleasure and pain. But when he is free of the body (when he knows himself different from the body), then neither pleasure nor pain touches him1."' i.

' " The wind is without body, the cloud, lightning, and thunder are without body (without hands, feet, etc.). Now as these, arising from this heavenly ether (space), appear in their own form, as soon as they have approached the highest light, 2.

' " Thus does that serene soul, arising from this body, appear in its own form, as soon as it has approached the highest light (the knowledge of Self2). He (in that state) is the highest person (uttama purusha). He moves about there laughing (or eating), playing, and rejoicing (in his mind), be it with women, carriages, or relatives, never minding that body into which he was born3.
' " Like as a horse attached to a cart, so is the spirit4 (prawa, pra^rnatman) attached to this body."' 3.
1 Ordinary, worldly pleasure. Comm.

2 The simile is not so striking as most of those old similes are. The wind is compared with the Self, on account of its being for a time lost in the ether (space), as the Self is in the body, and then rising again out of the ether and assuming its own form as wind The chief stress is laid on the highest light, which in the one case is the sun of summer, in the other the light of knowledge.

1 These are pleasures which seem hardly compatible with the state of perfect peace which the Self is supposed to have attained. The passage may be interpolated, or put in on purpose to show that the Self enjoys such pleasures as an inward spectator only, without identifying himself with either pleasure or pain. He sees them, as he says afterwards, with his divine eye. The Self perceives in all things his Self only, nothing else. In his commentary on the Taittiriya Upanishad (p. 45) /Sankara refers this passage to Brahman as an effect, not to Brahman as a cause.

4 The spirit is not identical with the body, but only joined to it, PHILOSOPHY AND RELIGION. 327 ' " Now where the sight has entered into the void (the open space, the black pupil of the eye), there is the person of the eye, the eye itself is the instrument of seeing. He who knows, let me smell this, he is the Self, the nose is the instrument of smelling. He who knows, let me say this, he is the Self, the tongue is the instrument of saying. He who knows, let me hear this, he is the Self, the ear is the instrument of hearing." ' 4.

' " He who knows, let me think this, he is the Self, the mind is his divine eye1. He, the Self, seeing these pleasures (which to others are hidden like a buried treasure of gold) through his divine eye, i. e. the mind, rejoices.

' " The Devas who are in the world of Brahman worship that Self (as taught by Pra^apati to Indra, and by Indra to the Devas). There all worlds are held by them, and all pleasures. He who knows that Self and understands it, obtains all worlds and all desires." Thus said Pra^apati, thus said Pra^apati.'
Y%navalkya and Maitreyi.
The next extract is taken from the Br^hadara?iyaka, where it is repeated twice, with slight differences, the first time in the second, the second time in the fourth Adhyaya 2 :

like a horse, or driving it, like a charioteer. In other passages the senses are the horses, buddhi, reason, the charioteer, manas, mind, the reins. The spirit is attached to the cart by the &etana : cf. Anandagrnanagiri.
1 Because it perceives not only what is present, but also what is past and future.
2 The variations of the second recension are marked by B.
' Yagmavalkya l had two wives, Maitreyi and Katyayani. Of these Maitreyi was conversant with Brahman, but Katyayani possessed such knowledge only as women possess.

' Now when Yagnavalkya was going to enter upon another state, he said : " Maitreyi, verily I am going away from this my house (into the forest)2. For sooth, let me make a settlement between thee and that Katyayani (my other wife).'" i.
' Maitreyi said : " My Lord, if this whole earth full of wealth belonged to me, tell me, should I be im mortal by it3?"
' " No," replied Yax/navalkya ; " like the life of rich people will be thy life. But there is no hope of im mortality by wealth/" 2.
' And Maitreyi said : " What should I do with that by which I do not become immortal \ What my Lord knoweth (of immortality), tell that to me4."' 3.
'Yacmavalkya replied : "Thou who art truly dear to me, thou speakest dear words. Come, sit down5, I will explain it to thee, and mark well what I say/" 4.
' And he said : " Verily a husband is not dear, that you may love the husband ; but that you may love the Self, therefore a husband is dear.
' " Verily a wife is not dear, that you may love the 1 This introductory paragraph occurs in the second version only.
zh: 在他回到諸提婆（Devas）之前，他便看見了這個困難。事實上，他既不認識自己（其自我）就是「我」，也不認識任何存在之物。他已歸於徹底的湮滅。我看不出這有何益處。』
『他手持薪柴，再次以弟子之身前往生主（Prajāpati）處。生主對他說：「摩伽梵（Maghavat），你既已心滿意足地離去，為何又回來？」』
他說：「先生，照那樣，他既不知道他自己（他的自我）就是『我』，也不知道任何存在的事物。他陷入了徹底的虛無。我看不出這有何益處。」
我將進一步向你解釋他（真實的自我），僅此而已¹。在此再住五年。」
『他在那裡又住了五年。這樣總共是一百零一年，因此據說因陀羅‧摩伽梵（Indra Maghavat）作為學生與生主（Prajāpati）同住了一百零一年。生主對他說：』
> 「摩伽梵，此身有死，常為死所持。此身乃彼無死、無形之『自我』（Self）之居所²。當『自我』住於身中（因思此身即我，我即此身），便為苦樂所縛。¹／商羯羅（Śaṅkara）解釋此句意指：真實之『自我』，非異於『自我』之任何他物。」
2. 根據某些觀點，身體是「自我」（Self）的產物，身體的元素——光、水與土——皆源自於「自我」，而「自我」隨後進入其中。
> 身體，他無法脫離快樂與痛苦。但當他脫離身體（當他認知到自己不同於身體）時，快樂與痛苦便都觸及不到他了¹。」¹
> 「風是無形的，雲、閃電與雷鳴亦是無形的（無手、足等）。如今這些，從這天穹之太虛（空間）升起，以自身之形顯現，一旦接近那至高的光明，
> 「如此，那寧靜之靈，從此身升起，一旦接近那至高之光（對真我的認識²），便顯現其自身之相。他（在此狀態中）即為至高之人（uttama purusha）。他在彼處嬉笑（或飲食）、遊玩、歡欣（於其心中），無論是與女子、車乘或親屬相伴，全不掛念那他所從出的身體³。」
「猶如馬匹繫於車乘，靈（prawa, pra^rnatman）亦如是繫於此身。」
1. 普通的、世俗的歡愉。註釋（Comm.）
2 這個比喻不如大多數古老比喻那般鮮明。風被比作自我（Self），因為它一度消融於以太（空間）之中，正如自我消融於身體之內，隨後又從以太中升起，恢復其作為風的自身形態。其主要重點落在最高的光上：在一個情況中是夏日的太陽，在另一個情況中則是知識之光。
1 這些喜悅似乎與「自我」理應已達成的圓滿寂靜狀態難以相容。此段落可能是後人插入，或特意加入以表明「自我」僅作為內在旁觀者享受此類喜悅，而不與苦樂認同。如後文所述，祂以神聖之眼觀照這一切。「自我」在萬物中僅感知到祂自身的「自我」，別無他物。商羯羅在其《泰帝利耶奧義書》註疏（第45頁）中，將此段落歸於作為果報的梵，而非作為因的梵。
4  精神不等同於身體，而只是與之結合。哲學與宗教。327「『當視線進入虛空（開闊空間、眼睛的黑色瞳孔）時，那裡便是眼之自我，眼睛本身只是觀看的工具。那個知道「讓我聞此物」者，便是自我，鼻子只是嗅聞的工具。那個知道「讓我說此話」者，便是自我，舌頭只是言說的工具。那個知道「讓我聽此聲」者，便是自我，耳朵只是聽聞的工具。』」
> 「知之者，容我思此，彼即自我（Self），心識乃其神聖之眼¹。彼，即自我，藉其神聖之眼——亦即心識——得見此等喜樂（對他人而言，此樂猶如埋藏之黃金寶藏），因而歡欣。」
> 「居住於梵界中的諸提婆（Devas）敬拜那自我（如生主教因陀羅、因陀羅再教諸提婆）。一切世界與一切喜樂皆為他們所持。凡了知此自我、通達此自我者，得獲一切世界與一切願望。」生主如是說，生主如是說。
Yājñavalkya 與 Maitreyī。
下一段引文選錄自《大森林奧義書》（Bṛhadāraṇyaka），該段落重複出現兩次，僅有細微差異：第一次見於第二〈阿提亞耶〉（Adhyāya），第二次見於第四〈阿提亞耶〉²。
像一匹馬，或像駕馭者驅策戰車。在其他段落中，感官是馬匹，*buddhi*（覺性）是駕馭者，*manas*（意）是韁繩。靈魂則透過*etana*（繫繩）與車身相連：參見Ānandagiri 註疏。
1. 因為它不僅感知當下，也感知過去與未來。
2 第二校勘本的異文以 B 標示。
'祭皮衣仙（Yājñavalkya）有兩位妻子，彌勒依（Maitreyī）與迦底耶尼（Kātyāyanī）。其中彌勒依通曉梵（Brahman），而迦底耶尼僅擁有婦女所具備的那種知識。
> 如今，當祭衣（Yagnavalkya）即將進入另一種狀態時，他說：「彌勒依（Maitreyi），誠然，我即將離開我這所房屋（前往森林）²。因此，讓我在妳與那位迦底亞尼（Katyayani，我的另一位妻子）之間做個了結吧。」¹
「彌勒耶說：『我主，若這遍滿財寶的整個大地歸我所有，請告訴我，我能因此獲得永生嗎？』」
'「不，」耶若婆佉（Yājñavalkya）回答；「你的生命將如富人的生活一般。但財富並不能帶來不朽的希望。」
'梅崔伊說：「那不能使我成為不朽之物，我要它何用？我主所知（關於不朽之事），請將那告訴我。」'
'Yacmavalkya 回答：「你這位真正為我所親愛的人，說出了親愛的話語。來，請坐下，我將為你解說，你要仔細聽我所言。」
> 他說：「誠然，丈夫並非因其本身而可愛，使你能愛丈夫；而是為了你能愛『自我』（Self），因此丈夫才顯得可愛。」
「『誠然，妻子並非因其可愛而令你愛她，』¹ （此開場段落僅見於第二版。）
